subject: EU Law
module_title: History and Development of the EU
lesson_title: From Ruin to Union: The Origins of European Integration

questions.question: Which declaration, made on 9 May 1950, is widely regarded as the foundational moment that led to European integration?
questions.explanation: The Schuman Declaration of 9 May 1950, proposed by French Foreign Minister Robert Schuman (inspired by Jean Monnet), proposed placing French and German coal and steel production under a common High Authority, leading to the ECSC.

questions.question: The European Coal and Steel Community (ECSC) was established by which treaty, and in which year did it enter into force?
questions.explanation: The ECSC was established by the Treaty of Paris, signed in April 1951, which entered into force in July 1952. It was a fifty-year treaty that expired in 2002.

questions.question: Which six states were the founding members of the European Economic Community established by the Treaty of Rome in 1957?

options.A: France, Germany, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg
options.B: France, Germany, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands, Denmark
options.C: France, Germany, Austria, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg
options.D: France, Germany, Italy, Spain, Netherlands, Luxembourg

questions.explanation: The six founding members (the 'Inner Six') were France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg — the same states that had formed the ECSC in 1951.

questions.question: The Treaty of Rome (1957) established two new Communities. Which were they?

options.C: The EEC and the Council of Europe

questions.explanation: The Treaty of Rome comprised two separate treaties signed on 25 March 1957: one establishing the European Economic Community (EEC) and one establishing the European Atomic Energy Community (Euratom/EAEC).

questions.question: What was the primary economic objective of the European Economic Community as established in 1957?

options.A: To establish a monetary union with a single currency
options.B: To create a common market with free movement of goods, persons, services and capital
options.C: To coordinate foreign and security policy among member states
options.D: To regulate nuclear energy production across member states

questions.explanation: The primary objective of the EEC was the establishment of a common market, involving the free movement of goods, persons, services, and capital, and the approximation of member states' economic policies.

questions.question: Jean Monnet's approach to European integration is best characterised as:

options.B: Functionalist — achieving integration step by step through economic cooperation
options.C: Intergovernmentalist — preserving national sovereignty above all
options.D: Supranationalist — transferring all powers immediately to a central authority

questions.explanation: Jean Monnet advocated a functionalist approach: economic cooperation in specific sectors would build trust, create interdependence, and gradually lead to deeper integration through a 'spill-over' effect — integration proceeding step by step rather than through a single constitutional leap.

questions.question: The Marshall Plan (European Recovery Programme) launched in 1948 is relevant to EU history because it:

options.B: Provided the economic impetus and demonstrated the value of American-style cooperation, encouraging European nations to work together
options.C: Required European states to adopt a federal constitution as a condition of aid
options.D: Created the Organisation for European Economic Cooperation which later became the EU

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan, offering US financial aid to war-devastated Europe, required recipient states to cooperate on its administration (through the OEEC). It demonstrated that economic cooperation between former enemies was possible and desirable, contributing to the political will to integrate.

questions.question: Which institution was NOT part of the original ECSC structure?
questions.explanation: The ECSC had four institutions: the High Authority (executive body), the Common Assembly (predecessor to the European Parliament), the Special Council of Ministers, and the Court of Justice. A European Central Bank was not established until the Maastricht Treaty era.

questions.question: The Merger Treaty of 1965 (Brussels Treaty) achieved what significant institutional development?

options.A: It merged the EEC and ECSC into a single Community
options.B: It merged the institutions of the three Communities (ECSC, EEC, Euratom) into single shared institutions
options.C: It merged the Council of Europe with the EEC
options.D: It established a single legislative procedure for all EU acts

questions.explanation: The Merger Treaty of 1965 (in force 1967) did not merge the three Communities themselves (each retained its own treaty base) but created a single Commission and single Council to serve all three Communities, rationalising the institutional structure.

questions.question: Why was coal and steel specifically chosen as the basis for the first Community?

options.A: They were the most profitable industries in post-war Europe
options.B: They were the key industries of war production — placing them under common control made future war between France and Germany materially impossible
options.C: The GATT required sectoral integration before a wider common market could be established
options.D: The United States insisted on coal and steel integration as a condition of the Marshall Plan

questions.explanation: Schuman and Monnet chose coal and steel deliberately: these were the basic industries of armaments production. By placing them under a common supranational authority, France and Germany (and others) made it structurally impossible for either to mobilise independently for war against the other.

questions.question: The Council of Europe, established in 1949, is relevant to EU law because:

options.A: It is the precursor institution to the European Council
options.B: It established the European Convention on Human Rights, which remains a separate legal system alongside EU law
options.C: It drafted the Treaty of Rome
options.D: It is an institution of the EU responsible for long-term strategic direction

questions.explanation: The Council of Europe (headquartered in Strasbourg) is entirely separate from the EU. It is responsible for the European Convention on Human Rights and the European Court of Human Rights. EU member states are also members of the Council of Europe, creating two parallel but distinct European human rights frameworks.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'empty chair crisis' of 1965-66?

options.A: The European Parliament's refusal to attend sessions in protest at the lack of democratic scrutiny
options.B: France's boycott of Council meetings in protest at proposed majority voting and increased Community powers over agriculture
options.C: Germany's refusal to participate in ECSC proceedings following a dispute over coal quotas
options.D: The failure of the Constitutional Treaty referendum, leaving the EU without a constitutional framework

questions.explanation: The 'empty chair crisis' arose when France (under de Gaulle) refused to attend Council meetings from June 1965, objecting to proposals to fund the Community from its own resources and to extend qualified majority voting. It was resolved by the Luxembourg Compromise (1966), which preserved a de facto veto for states on matters of vital national interest.

questions.question: The Luxembourg Compromise of 1966 established:

options.A: A formal right of veto for all member states on all Council decisions
options.B: A political agreement that where a member state declared a very important national interest at stake, the Council would endeavour to reach unanimous agreement
options.C: The principle that qualified majority voting would apply to all internal market legislation
options.D: A requirement that the European Parliament consent to all major Community decisions

questions.explanation: The Luxembourg Compromise was a political (not legally binding) agreement: where a member state declared that very important national interests were at stake, the Council would endeavour to reach unanimous agreement. France interpreted this as a permanent veto; other states treated it more narrowly. It significantly slowed integration by entrenching unanimity in practice.
